nativeRange.habitat: Found in slow-moving and standing waters including flood plains, lakes, and river tributaries associated with the Paraná, Paraguay, and Uruguay river basins, including the Pantanal wetland regions.

diet.notes: Readily accepts dried flakes, micro-pellets, and frozen or live foods such as bloodworm, Daphnia, and brine shrimp. Vegetable matter — such as algae-based flakes or blanched spinach — is a beneficial addition. The Red Eye Tetra is generally considered one of the less finicky tetras when it comes to diet.

imageCandidates.notes: Cedricguppy - Loury Cedric, 4032x2272. Tack-sharp aquarium shot; vivid red iridescent eye, bold black caudal-peduncle bar and silver/green body clearly shown, multiple fish framing the schooling species.
Cedricguppy - Loury Cedric, 4032x2272. Sharp group shot, different composition; red eye and black tail-base bar crisp on two fully-framed adults.
Yercaud-elango, 1800x1996. Full shoal of ~10 fish showing schooling behaviour and diagnostic red eyes / black caudal bars; slightly soft focus so kept as non-recommended context shot.

summary: The Red Eye Tetra is a hardy, active schooling fish from South American river basins, recognisable by its silvery body, vivid red iridescent eye, and bold black-and-yellow caudal peduncle markings. It is generally recommended for beginners owing to its tolerance of a wide range of water conditions.
careNotes: The Red Eye Tetra (Moenkhausia sanctaefilomenae) is generally considered one of the hardier tetras and tends to be a good choice for community tanks, provided tankmates are chosen carefully. Keep in groups of at least 6, and ideally 8–10; smaller groups have been known to increase fin-nipping tendency. Long-finned species such as Angelfish, Bettas, and fancy Guppies are commonly recommended to be avoided as tankmates due to fin-nipping behaviour. With similarly robust, active fish they tend to do well. The tank should offer open swimming space in the middle zone, with denser planting and driftwood around the margins for cover. They are reportedly not demanding about water chemistry in captivity, though soft to moderately hard water in the mid-pH range appears to suit them well.
breedingNotes: Breeding is considered moderately difficult for the average hobbyist and is typically done in a dedicated spawning tank. A separate tank roughly 45 × 30 × 30 cm with softer, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–7.0, gH 1–10, temperature around 26–29°C) is commonly recommended. Eggs are scattered among fine-leaved plants or a mesh and tend to be cannibalized by the parents, so adults should generally be removed after spawning. Eggs typically hatch within 24–36 hours and fry become free-swimming after a further 3–4 days. First foods are usually infusoria, progressing to microworm and baby brine shrimp nauplii. Breeding groups of 6 males and 6 females, or pairs, have both been reported to work.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Used as primary source after Aquarium Co-Op species page returned HTTP 404. SeriouslyFish is the first listed secondary for the fish-freshwater slice and provides comprehensive husbandry and taxonomy data.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (7 cm), temperature (22–26°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (5–19 dH), and native range. FishBase now lists this species under genus Bario in family Acestrorhamphidae/Thayeriinae following a recent molecular revision; however Moenkhausia sanctaefilomenae / Characidae remains the consensus name in the aquarium hobby. Minimum tank noted as 80 cm length.

additional.name: Aquarium Store Depot
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (22–26°C), pH (6–8), hardness (5–20 dH), adult size (up to ~7.5 cm), lifespan (3–5 years), diet, fin-nipping behaviour, and care level. Confirms avoid with Bettas, Angelfish, fancy Guppies.

fish.breedingNotes: An egg-scatter spawner that will eat its own eggs; adults should be removed from the spawning tank promptly after spawning. A dedicated breeding tank with soft, slightly acidic water is required. Fry are small and need infusoria as a first food before graduating to larger fare.
